summary: Incision of the duct followed by manual removal is recommended for managing submandibular calculi, especially if they are over 5 mm; this approach balances effectiveness and invasiveness based on current medical guidelines and research findings. To address specific case complexities not fitting standard protocols, alternative strategies such as sialendoscopy may also play crucial roles depending upon individual circumstances including stone size, mobility, anatomical variations, and ongoing symptomatic issues unresponsive to less intrusive measures. In summary, the primary recommendation centers around utilizing ductal access combined with physical extraction tailored appropriately across varying degrees of difficulty presented within typical clinical encounters dealing with refractory salivary gland disorders stemming primarily but not exclusively from impacted calcified masses located beneath oral mucosa near mandible region boundaries demarcated clinically via palpation examinations supplemented further through diagnostic imaging modalities whenever necessary during preoperative planning phases aimed ultimately toward optimizing outcomes achieved postoperatively without compromising long-term quality-of-life expectations held forth prospectively among affected individuals seeking definitive resolution of their condition(s). Agent2 concludes after reviewing multiple sources that incision of the duct along with manual removal should generally be preferred unless other non-surgical interventions have been deemed sufficient
